cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 55-year-old is brought to ED unwell with febrile, with respiratory distress, and with pneumonia.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 26-year-old presents with acute dyspnoea, dizziness, and hand paraesthesiae with a normal cardiorespiratory examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~20.8; measured 20.6 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 40-year-old presents with low GCS and hypoventilation after being found collapsed at home.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.9; measured 27.8 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~30.5 (+/-2); measured 31.1 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (114 + 15) = 9, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old is brought to ED with hypotensive and with rigors.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old presents with rapid breathing, light-headedness, and perioral tingling after an acute stressful event.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.0; measured 21.5 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 30-year-old is brought to ED with dehydrated and tachypnoeic. Associated features include after missed insulin doses and with persistent vomiting.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 67-year-old presents with dizziness and volume depletion after recurrent nausea and retching.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~46.3; measured 44.9 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.

cases.title: Lactic acidosis (sepsis)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 29-year-old presents with light-headedness and dehydration after several days of gastroenteritis symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~38.0 (+/-2); measured 36.7 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (107 + 20) = 11, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old smoker presents with worsening dyspnoea, productive cough, and chronic exercise limitation.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~32.4; measured 32.7 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~32.8; measured 33.2 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (139 - (103 + 16) = 20) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~29.0 (+/-2); measured 30.3 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (115 + 14) = 9, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 18-year-old is brought to ED with recent weight loss, rapid breathing, nausea and abdominal discomfort, and dehydration.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~23.0 (+/-2); measured 23.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (94 + 10) = 32 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: COPD exacerbation (acute-on-chronic respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 76-year-old comes to ED with increased work of breathing and a history of COPD. Associated features include increased sputum and hypercapnic symptoms.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: COPD exacerbations cause acute rises in CO2 on a background of chronic respiratory acidosis.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 19-year-old is brought to ED after several days of illness, with deep rapid breathing and nausea and abdominal discomfort.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~32.0 (+/-2); measured 31.8 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (97 + 16) = 23 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 18-year-old comes to ED with vomiting and recent weight loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (+/-2); measured 28.1 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (100 + 13) = 25 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: Vomiting (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 67-year-old presents with dizziness and volume depletion after recurrent nausea and retching.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise metabolic alkalosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~50.5; measured 51.5 is appropriate, supporting metabolic alkalosis with expected respiratory compensation, as seen with vomiting.
